practice: Configure a browser to send traffic through ZAP.
Explain how passive scanning differs from active scanning.
Identify two types of messages that passive scanning inspects.
title: ZAP Lesson 4: Proxying and Passive Scan
summary: Route traffic through ZAP and understand how passive scanning works.
content:
# ZAP Lesson 4: Proxying and Passive Scan

## Overview
ZAP can proxy your browser so it records requests and responses while you explore the target. Passive scanning then analyzes this traffic in the background.

## Proxying basics
- Configure your browser to use ZAP as an HTTP/HTTPS proxy
- Use the built-in browser from Quick Start for a preconfigured setup
- Explore the application to populate the Sites tree

## Passive scanning
- Runs in the background on observed traffic
- Does not modify requests or responses
- Scans HTTP and WebSocket messages to raise alerts

## Why it matters
Passive scanning is safe and can run continuously while you browse or test. It surfaces issues without actively attacking the target.
examples: Launch the built-in browser to avoid manual proxy configuration.
Review passive scan alerts after a manual walkthrough.
Use passive scanning to catch missing security headers.
objectives: Describe how passive scanning analyzes traffic.
Identify where passive alerts appear in the UI.
Explain why passive scanning is safe to run.
